retrieved_docs.title: The Effect of Pupil Size on Visual Resolution -- Issues of Concern -- Visual Axis, Optical Axis, and Angle Kappa
retrieved_docs.content: The human eye has 2 significant optical axes – the optical and visual axes, often referred to in the literature as the pupillary axis and the line of sight, respectively. The optical axis comprises an imaginary line perpendicular to the cornea, intersecting the center of the entrance pupil. [32] On the other hand, the visual axis is an imaginary line that connects the object in space, the centers of the entrance and exit pupils, and the foveal center. The visual axis is also known as the foveal-fixation axis. [61]

retrieved_docs.title: Traumatic Cataract -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The physical evaluation of a traumatic cataract aims to determine if surgery is necessary and, if so, which procedure will be safest and most effective. [20] [41] [42] When assessing acute ocular trauma, a thorough evaluation is essential to determine the severity of the eye injury. [2] [20] The physical examination should carefully evaluate visual acuity, pupillary function, and intraocular pressure. Slit lamp biomicroscopy and a dilated fundus examination should be performed. Identifying signs of zonular damage, such as phacodonesis, focal iridodonesis, vitreous prolapse, and lens subluxation, is crucial though these signs may not always be present (see Image. Traumatic Cataract). [20] [43] Subtle signs of lens injury include seeing the lens equator during eccentric gaze, a decentered nucleus in the primary position, an iridolenticular gap, or changes in the lens periphery contour. The formation of a cataract within minutes to hours after ocular trauma may indicate a violation of the anterior lens capsule. [44]

retrieved_docs.title: The Effect of Pupil Size on Visual Resolution -- Issues of Concern -- Visual Axis, Optical Axis, and Angle Kappa
retrieved_docs.content: When considering using a multifocal intraocular lens (MFIOL) in cataract surgery, it may be beneficial to consider the angle kappa, especially for hyperopic patients with a large angle kappa. Eyes with a larger angle kappa tend to exhibit more glare and halos after cataract surgery with the placement of MFIOLs. [65] Several methods are proposed to accommodate for a large angle kappa in cataract surgery with MFIOL, including decentering the MFIOL towards the visual axis and gluing 1 haptic in place, centering the capsulorhexis on the coaxially sighted corneal light reflex, and postoperative pupilloplasty with argon laser to center the pupil more closely to the visual axis. [66] [67]

retrieved_docs.title: Phacoemulsification -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Post-occlusion Surge
retrieved_docs.content: This phenomenon occurs when the occlusion is broken, and fluid moves from the anterior chamber (high-pressure zone) to aspiration tubing (low pressure). Surge can also lead to anterior chamber collapse and cause the iris and posterior capsule to be aspirated towards the port. It can be prevented by increasing fluid influx, alleviating vacuum, small diameter coiled tubing, and occlusion mode software. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Fluorescein Angiography -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: The pupil should be well dilated before starting the procedure. The patient should be primed about the risk of nausea so that initial images are properly acquired. The emergency cart with the equipment should be available. The lens of the camera should be checked for clarity, and alignment and focusing should be cross-verified. A few control images should be taken to check for pseudo-fluorescence and autofluorescence. The patient's arm should be placed comfortably on the armrest, and the patient should be comfortably seated. The chin should be placed on the chin rest. Explaining to the patient that the images will be taken in primary gaze and then in different gazes (for a usual fundus camera) makes the procedure swift. The scalp vein set is to be placed in the patient's arm, and some blood is drawn into the tubing to confirm the position of the needle.

retrieved_docs.title: Eye Trauma Imaging -- Anatomy
retrieved_docs.content: The lens is suspended by zonules connected to the ciliary body and divides the globe into the anterior and posterior segments. Traumatic violation of the lens zonules may cause dislocation of the lens. The iris and pupil are anterior to the lens and subdivide the anterior segment into the anterior and posterior chambers, which are both filled with aqueous humor. Posterior to the lens is a large cavity filled with vitreous humor.

retrieved_docs.title: The Effect of Pupil Size on Visual Resolution -- Clinical Significance -- Perceptual Influence of Pupil Size
retrieved_docs.content: The Asahi illusion is an image that, at first glance, resembles a glowing sun, consisting of a series of petal-like shapes surrounding a center. The petals follow a gradient from a yellow hue at the center to black at the edges, with the optical illusion design creating the impression that the center is significantly brighter than the surrounding areas. Despite the static nature of the image, the combination of the color gradient and the petal shape provides the impression that the white center is brighter than it is in reality. Remarkably, Laeng and his colleagues found that upon viewing the Asahi illusion, participants' pupils would constrict as though reacting to an actual increase in light intensity. This finding suggests that the mere perception of brightness, even without an actual increase in light, can lead to a physiological response in pupil size. [118]

retrieved_docs.title: Local and Regional Anesthesia in Ophthalmology and Ocular Trauma -- Technique or Treatment -- Targeted Nerve Blocks
retrieved_docs.content: It is important to draw back the syringe to ensure the needle is not in a vessel. Approximately 3 to 5 ml of anesthetic is injected into this space, paying attention to the expected anterior displacement of the globe, signifying injection of anesthetic into the intraconal space, and palpation of the globe to assess for posterior pressure. The needle is then withdrawn, and pressure is applied to the globe using gauze for 60 to 120 seconds to tamponade potential retrobulbar hemorrhage.

retrieved_docs.title: Retrobulbar Block -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Several anatomical landmarks have been described for the second injection, including the medial canthus, tear caruncle, and medial third of the superior orbital rim (nasal superior site). However, there are some important considerations to review: The insertion of the needle through the nasal superior site must be avoided. At this level, the distance between the orbital roof and the ocular globe is reduced, increasing the risk of ocular globe perforation and superior oblique damage. Instead, a nasal inferior injection should be attempted. Insertion of the needle for a second puncture should be no deeper than 25 mm, as this increases the risk of injecting a local anesthetic into the retrobulbar compartment and subsequent optic nerve damage. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Traumatic Glaucoma -- Pathophysiology -- Lens Subluxation and Dislocation +/- Secondary Pupil Block
retrieved_docs.content: Ocular trauma can cause posterior displacement of the lens-iris diaphragm, subsequently rupturing the zonular fibers that hold the lens in place. A lens may be subluxated when greater than 25% of the zonules are torn, such that the lens is partially displaced from its normal centralized position. Lens dislocation occurs when there is a complete disruption of all or most of the zonules. The lens can then become dislocated into the anterior chamber through the pupil or into the vitreous cavity. IOP elevation can occur via several mechanisms, including pupil block with intermittent or acute angle-closure by a subluxated lens, the release of pigments from chafing of a subluxated or dislocated lens on the iris, and trabecular meshwork blockage from vitreous strands prolapsed into the AC, in the setting of a frankly dislocated lens into the vitreous cavity. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Anisocoria -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Once a thorough history has been taken, the abnormal pupil needs to be identified. To do this, both pupil sizes should be measured with the room lights on brightly and then again with the room lights dim. Having the room lights completely off may not be practical since the pupils need to be visible for measurement. To measure the pupil, a small ruler may be used. There are also near cards that have pupil sizes on them for comparison. While measuring the pupils, the patient should focus on a distant object. This is to avoid miosis (pupillary constriction) while the patient is accommodating at a near target. After the pupils have been measured, the practitioner should identify whether the anisocoria is greater in light or darker conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: Autorefractors -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Optometer Principle
retrieved_docs.content: Based on the target's position, light from the target situated on the far side of the lens enters the eyes with vergers of varying amounts. The light vergence at the focal plane of the optometer lens is directly related to the target's displacement. Hence a scale can be plotted with equal spacing showing the dioptres of correction needed. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Enophthalmos -- History and Physical -- Clinical Measurement: Exophthalmometry
retrieved_docs.content: Naugle exophthalmometer: is a similar, horizontally placed bar between two lateral canthi; it takes this a step further by using a 4-point fixation system, involving the superior and inferior orbital margins. Black markers along the length of the instrument aid in finding the midpoint of the pupils, thereby allowing the user to easily reposition the instrument for a serial of reproducible measurements. A study comparing Hertel and Naugle exophthalmometry found Naugle measurements to be more reliable in those who have suffered orbito-zygomatic fractures, where the lateral border of the orbit is disturbed, and the patient experience is more pleasant. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: The Effect of Pupil Size on Visual Resolution -- Issues of Concern -- Impact of Corectopia and Polycoria on Vision
retrieved_docs.content: The precise location of the pupil within the iris, known as the pupil center, is critical for optimal vision. When the pupil is decentered or displaced from its normal central position, it can profoundly affect visual function. A decentered pupil is termed corectopia. [52]

Together, these entities and relationships highlight the importance of precise anatomic localization to the eye and its lens capsule for accurate diagnosis, surgical planning, and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1968
retrieved_docs.content: Operative procedure on anterior segment of eye is directly related to the eye (oči), indicating that surgical interventions targeting the front structures of the eye are procedures performed on the ocular organ.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2372
retrieved_docs.content: One stage intracapsular extraction of lens by inferior temporal route and insertion of prosthetic intraocular lens is an ocular surgical technique that is designated as a procedure_site_of the eye. The operative procedure on anterior segment of eye has relationship to the eye (oči). Both interventions involve direct manipulation of ocular structures, underscoring that the eye serves as the anatomical location for these procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 156
retrieved_docs.content: الكاذب لمحفظة العدسة (pseudo‑exfoliation of the lens capsule). Surgical management includes procedures such as Extraction of after cataract with capsulotomy, which generates the child procedure إخراج العدسة (lens extraction); the procedure category “lens and cataract procedures” is parented by إخراج العدسة and targets the lens capsule as the direct procedural site. Extraction of primary membranous cataract by excision similarly leads to إخراج العدسة and is a member of اضطرابات العدسة. The one‑stage intracapsular extraction of the lens by inferior temporal route with insertion of a prosthetic intra‑ocular lens is defined as a lens and cataract procedure, underscoring the central role of lens capsule manipulation. This intra‑ocular procedure is performed within the eye, establishing a procedure_site_of relationship to the ocular structure, while the finding التقشّر الكاذب لمحفظة العدسة (pseudo‑erosion of the lens capsule) is localized to the lens capsule via a has_finding_site

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2710
retrieved_docs.content: Atropine and cyclopentolate are both classified as mydriatics, a group of agents that produce pupil dilation. As mydriatic drugs, they are used in ophthalmic practice to achieve mydriasis for diagnostic examinations, therapeutic procedures, and the management of certain eye conditions, reflecting their shared pharmacologic role in facilitating ocular assessment and treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 954
retrieved_docs.content: Ibopamine and cyclopentolate are both classified as mydriatics, meaning they are pharmacologic agents used to dilate the pupil. As mydriatic drugs, they act on the iris muscles to produce pupil enlargement, which is valuable in ophthalmic examinations and certain therapeutic procedures. Their shared classification underscores a common mechanism of action and similar clinical applications in eye care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 811
retrieved_docs.content: One stage intracapsular extraction of lens by inferior temporal route and insertion of prosthetic intraocular lens is classified as a lens and cataract procedure and is performed on the eye.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 823
retrieved_docs.content: Intracapsular extraction of lens is classified as a type of lens and cataract procedure, indicating that it belongs to the broader group of surgical interventions aimed at treating lens pathology and cataract formation. This procedure can be organized according to the “Operations by approach” framework, reflecting that the surgical technique is defined by the anatomical route used to access the lens. A more detailed variant, the one‑stage intracapsular extraction of lens by inferior temporal route and insertion of a prosthetic intraocular lens, is also identified as a lens and cataract procedure. This specific operation combines removal of the lens through an inferior temporal approach with immediate implantation of an artificial intraocular lens, and it is performed within the eye (procedure site of the eye). Together, these relationships illustrate how intracapsular lens extraction procedures are categorized, differentiated by surgical approach, and linked to their anatomical site of execution, providing a clear hierarchy and contextual understanding for clinicians planning cataract surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1628
retrieved_docs.content: Cykloplegika are used as mydriatics and cycloplegics, serving to dilate the pupil and temporarily paralyze accommodation during ophthalmic examinations and procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2029
retrieved_docs.content: One stage intracapsular extraction of the lens by inferior temporal route and insertion of prosthetic intraocular lens is a surgical procedure that is performed on the eye.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: detachment of the eye (انفصال مشيمية العين) and hemorrhagic choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي). Peripheral involvement includes peripheral disseminated choroiditis with chorioretinitis and peripheral focal retinitis with retinochoroiditis. Across all entities, the choroidea (choroid) is the anatomical locus of the characteristic lesions, underscoring its central role in pathophysiology, clinical assessment, precise diagnosis, and tailored management of choroidal and retinal disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 1174
retrieved_docs.content: Intracapsular extraction of lens and One stage intracapsular extraction of lens by inferior temporal route and insertion of prosthetic intraocular lens are both classified as lens and cataract procedures. The one‑stage technique is a specific variant that utilizes an inferior temporal surgical route and includes placement of a prosthetic intraocular lens; its operative site is the eye (oči). Operative procedure on anterior segment of eye is also linked to the eye, indicating that these lens‑related surgeries are performed on the anterior segment of the ocular structure. Together, these concepts describe a group of cataract‑related interventions that target the lens within the eye, emphasizing the procedural variations and anatomical focus relevant to ophthalmic surgical care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1579
retrieved_docs.content: Anticholinergic mydriatics and cycloplegics are classified as a type of mydriatics and cycloplegics, agents used to dilate the pupil and temporarily paralyze the eye’s accommodative ability.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 235
retrieved_docs.content: is treated surgically by procedures such as trabeculectomy (استئصال التربيق), angle surgery (بضع الزاوية), and other anterior‑segment operations that are children of the broader category “glaucoma procedures.” Facilitation of intra‑ocular circulation is a parent concept for these glaucoma procedures, emphasizing the importance of maintaining aqueous outflow. Overall, the community’s knowledge links cataract and glaucoma as lens‑related and anterior‑segment disorders of the eye, connects their anatomical sites (ocular lens and lens capsule) to specific surgical interventions, and integrates antiglaucoma pharmacotherapy—particularly karbachol—into the therapeutic framework for managing intra‑ocular pressure and aqueous dynamics.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1262

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 0 -- Community 18
retrieved_docs.content: serves as a parent activity within the broader set of glaucoma‑related surgeries. Glaucoma (زَرَق) is a subgroup of the angle‑related surgery category (بضع الزاوية) and includes procedures such as trabeculectomy (استئصال التربيق). Operative procedures on the anterior segment of the eye are linked to the eye and have a broader relationship with glaucoma procedures. The relationship between facilitation of intraocular circulation and زَرَق suggests a role in ocular blood‑flow regulation.
